pages.text:
at Ulm. Jewish window, Goose poking gargoyle, choir carvings, central altar. American Meier designed modern town hall. Difficult lunch - making decisions in a foreign language.
	We drove on through lovely grassy fields of Bavaria to __Ellessee__ an old campsite on a lake.
	Aitrang / Allgau
	It's well laid out with good clean showers. You have to pay to swim in the lake. Art classes - arty things round campsite, Metal work, glass, poems! Lights in showers painted. Clean air. Enormous stones marking levels of pitches. What was there originally? Fresh bread at breakfast. It rained in the evening.
	~~Friday~~ Thursday -
	We pass dinky red trains. Bavaria is very beautiful. Hot terrible traffic jams caused by trivial roadworks. German motorway, too dirty. Late morning we pass thro Austria. Past Innsbruck. on to Bolsano. A long day's driving. We keep pink Dolomite Mountains on our left. Fruit trees. V. fertile valley. Castle.
	Lake Garda comes in sight & we join the

captions: tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it — photo of campsite/transit area on lake shore
pages.text:
queue of traffic down the hill. We try 4 different campsites, all full. In the end we go to [?Bolsari] Transit which we think will be just a lay by but instead it turns out to be a Check-in/post site just for camping vans. Amazing. No lights at night. Only the van width next to us for table & chairs. Sat nice people. The van opposite have a kid russel + border terrier. They give us their fresh left-over bread for our supper. The woman next to us has a 10 year old son, Felix. I've never seen a single parent woman camping before, on her own. She's from Dresden. She teaches circus, she [illegible] in Sheffield + London.
	She + son with [illegible]
	[?Gloucester]
	[?Winchsea]
	[illegible]
	[illegible]
	[sticker: tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it — photo of campsite/transit area on lake shore]
	Friday we [?sat] after breakfast. V. beautiful.

off in the van left at Garda, [?Affri], back road to N. __Verona__. Difficult to find way. Van GPS slow. We come up a road marked ~~metres~~ 2.1m. The van is 2m. So we had
5cm spare
The van only just scrapes thro. I was anxious 3 roads before the end. Simon only got anxious at the very end. Ivy.
__Camping Castel San Pietro__ * * * * Incredibly hot. We have a shower, eat + [?fix] Simon joins queue for a tent pitch. We have to leave the van on the road. Next to motor cyclists who smoke out on the terraces.
     It's part of a [?B] castle with lots of terraces, higgledy piggledy.
                               Verona Opera

The motor cyclists turn out to be really nice. we give them tea + rock cakes on the terrace. Then we set off down the hill, after a second shower. We visit the cathedral + see Romanesque carvings, a few. We sit outside + watch very
[?Stage] for Carmen
[photo]
posh people dressed to the Nines, going to the opera. Wonderful style + clothes. One very fat lady is dressed in a plain white linen dress + you can see all her fat quivering as she moves. A good supper. Cold veg soup. Croutons with little dollops of cream [?cheese]! Then reglets cooked in myrtle! Delicious but rich.

The opera was incredible. 9.15 – 1.05. 4 Acts + 20 minute intervals. There were 6 horses + 3 donkeys. A very good Carmen. The tenor was a

[postcard: panoramic view of Verona with river; ticket stub reads: ROBERT CAPA / Biglietto: Intero / Prezzo: / Valido il 29/07/2012 Emesso il 28/07/2012 alle 19.21 / Comune di Verona Cultura]

substitute. It was horribly hot + sticky. People kept being ill. The very nice, unsophisticated Russian woman next to me started for to drink water + fan abt, gave a groan + passed out. Men with bright

[postcard: aerial view of Museo di Castelvecchio, Verona; Comune di Verona Assessorato alla Cultura]

orange trousers dashed around but it took them ages to reach us. Russia came round after we had splashed lots of water on her after about 5 minutes. She looked ghastly, pale, with owl eyes + running mascara. A nearby woman said we shd. get her lying down with her feet up, but the seating was so steep that we didn't dare move her. She refused to leave with the orange men. But after one more act they left. Then another woman 2 rows behind fainted. Unbearably hot until 3rd act. Walking home, up the long hill to the campsite, was exhausting + I didn't feel well with the heat.

    The [the] German motorcyclists only got back after us because he got lost. He's just completed his pilot's training. I hope he flies better than he walks.

Saturday Too hot in the van at breakfast. Too hot every minute of the day + evening. So we are missing Palladio in N. Italy + heading for the Alps near France. 5 minutes after a shower we pour sweat again.

    We walk down into the town [Verona] + see the

lovely squares, 2 squares. A whale bone is suspended in the passage between them. It will fall when a person walks thro whose never told a lie. We also go to see a wonderful Robert Capa exhibition & the city museum with beautiful paintings.

     The town is very, very attractive. The houses have [sketch of window frame] wide stone frames around their windows [sketch of window frame] & the planners have got shopowners to put wide stone frames around the plate glass windows, too. The kerbs of the pavements are often flush with the road.

     We catch a taxi back to the campsite & buy the excellent yoghurt & Simon brings us lunch on the lower terrace. We nap & I wash clothes. While I wash, a Dutch woman, who's come to refill her water bottle, stops to chat & it made clothes washing very enjoyable to just chat while I did it....

     R. Whelan wrote about Capa "Capa declared he was willing to risk his life to cover only those wars in which he loved one side & hated the other. Steinbeck: "He could show the horror of a whole people in the face of a child."

     We are hot in the night : sweat streaming.

Sunday 6.40. wake up. Get up even with tea to avoid ghastly temperature. Temperature OK until after breakfast. We take down tent & drive to St. Zeno. Lovely pink stones & red & white stripes. Carvings! [?Verotgiano]. [?Mantegna].

     (There were fossils in the pink steps at the campsite.)

     A beautiful [?durate] cloister with delicate columns & a loggia in it. Welcoming.

     We found a good, cheap supermarket; & bought lots of food, having almost run out. We drive WNW.

     [Lago] Orta   [Lago] Maggiore   [Lago] Como         [Lago] Garda

are the lakes of N. Italy. Lake Orta is the smallest & least known, only 13K top to bottom. There's a peninsula with a village called Orto San Giulio & the Island at the end is called Isola San Giulio. It's tiny & covered in buildings & has lots of [crossed out word] nuns, in enclosed order, average age 40. The village & surrounding hill sides are beautiful. We are lucky to get a [?site] in Camp Cusio [?*****] owned by a family whose [?father] [?niche] inherited the farm

although his children grew up in Peru. They are still developing the site. There are big static tents + log cabins, a pool, a cafe. Very welcoming, too. Lots of walks. Perfect temperature. Lots of Dutch + some Italians. No Germans or other Brits?

  We walk along the dangerous road to the beach + swim.
  In the night I storm out of bed + ask our neighbour to keep quiet. He's chatting loudly on his mob at 1.45. am in his van + the sound's funnelled out by his awning. Finally I slide his van door shut.
  __Monday__ I do a wash, or rather it's done for me for 4 Euros + we walk over the peninsula + look round the village /town + walk back along the coastal path. Lots of fierce dog signs - the rich protecting their property. It's too hot at the end. Lunch in the shade, peaceful afternoon reading. Another trek to the pleasant beach.

In the evening we have spaghetti at the café + amaretti biscuits back at the van.
  __Tuesday__
  I wash more clothes. We read in the morning. In the afternoon we climb the hill on the penninsula + admired the view out [to]
[photographs]
the island + of surrounding hills.
  We also wandered around the 20 chapels dedicated to the story of St. Francis. The chapels were built from about 1590-
to 1790. They have frescoed walls & posture figures, life size. The chapels are simple & beautiful & set in the grounds in a pleasing way. It's calm

captions: Città di Domodossola / Assessorato alla Cultura / Apre i suoi Musei
pages.text:
Afterwards we walked down to the village & had [?pnjjos] in the square.

__Wednesday__
We spend the morning in camp. My tummy's bad, so in the afternoon Simon goes off on a train trip on his own.

I travelled 47 km north from St. [?Gialgio] to Domodossola which had an old centre dating from the Middle Ages. The streets still showed the pattern of its five walls.

It acquired a new life when Napoleon chose the Simplon pass as a main route from France to Italy, and yet again [decoration] with the building [of]

the Simplon railway tunnel, still one of the longest in the world.  Simon's return rail journey was interrupted when the guard became ill and all passengers were turfed off the train.  A curious episode occurred when a local woman shouted from across about seven tracks at one of my fellow passengers. She walked across two more lines and the man cursed others in response to her shouting.  She then crossed back again just before a fast express from Milan thundered through the station.  The man returned saying she was mad, but a few minutes later a carabiniero arrived and called the man over, much to the consternation of my fellow passengers who felt an affinity for the man.  After he had gone (via a subway) to the policeman they both reappeared and the policeman seemed to get evidence from another traveller — he ignored the woman and only spoke to a middle aged man!  Then the policeman and the accused departed and the rest of us settled down to wait for the next train.

Thursday
Simon cycled up to Vacciago to look at an exhibition of modern art but it turned out

to have changed its opening hours and was closed.  The swooping cycle ride down was exhilarating.

Friday
Eve's stomach ailment was receding after two days of starvation and a Diocalm pill so we set off quite early to drive north and then went up a side valley to the foot of Monte Rosa.  We parked in a concrete fenced part reserved for camping vans and took a skilift in two stages from Macugnaga to Belvedere from which we walked over a ridge to see the Monte Rose glacier.  This was disappointing as the glacier is completely covered in boulders and pebbles that it carries down the mountain like a carapace against the sun.  On closer examination, though, the view was fascinating as you could see that the glacier had been larger and one could make out the huge "U" shape that it had recently carved out of the landscape.

Eve was not well enough to go further walking so Simon left her to descend via the skilift while he crossed a side offshoot of the glacier to climb up the edge of the "U" to a chalet Zamboni.

pages.sketch_desc: Hand-drawn sketch at top of page showing a mountain scene with arrows. Labels include: 'Sun' (top centre), 'Monte Rosa' (top right), 'footpath' (centre, with arrow pointing down to glacier), 'glacier' (centre-right), 'Zamboni' (left, with arrow pointing to a small building/hut symbol), 'open river' (bottom left). Arrows indicate the route/relationship between features.
pages.text:
[route sketch at top of page — see route_sketch_description]

open river                    The glacier appeared to have created a giant elevated causeway on its SE flank as the sun melted that side and forced it to drop boulders and moraine like a huge dam that then kept the glacier clinging to the side of the valley.
Simon had lunch at Zamboni in blinding sunlight. Occasionally he glimpsed M. Rosa through clouds towering impossibly above. There were many hanging glaciers high up the mountain.
 After lunch, he climbed up the side moraine to a cairn where a Dutch woman was seated. When he took out his camera she said they had forgotten to put the SD card so had no pictures of their mountain adventure so Simon took a few of her family and wrote his email address in her iPhone so that later she could send her email address and he could send her the photos.

captions: View of Monterosa (2nd highest mtn of Europe) from Amo[illegible] near lake d'Orta
pages.text:
[glued-in photograph of Monte Rosa / alpine landscape]

Very twisty roads on the way back to the campsite. We stopped at Bogognia where there was an old castle built to keep the Swiss away from Lake Maggiore. Then

captions: Joseph Albers. Study to homage the Square 1956. In the museum of Antonio + Carmela Calderara. Ameno.
pages.text: it became a prison (there was an oubliette) & is now a museum & a place for meetings. There was an exhibition of giant photos of insects. Stag beetles etc. The town still has the richness of centuries ago in some of its buildings & main streets. Pleasantly has- been. We returned to the camp & bought good chips to go with supp[er]

Saturday I got another load of wash done which has just about dried before 4pm rain. Humid. We drive along old roads to Vacciago (which was open this time) & saw the Calderara museum, a bit like Kettles Yard. Rather sad, as their only daughter had died, His

early paintings were [?some] figurative, later ones abstract. We like his work, but friends, whose work he had collected, seemed rather derivative. Beautiful old house with lovely views of lake.

      We stopped on our way to the van at a splendid corner shop. The owner gave me a book on local sights & post cards.

      We came back to the camp for lunch. I like this camp very much. They seem to welcome children & people of all ages, & dogs. There are group of children of various ages messing about all the time. 2 brothers seem to own it. It was their father's house, he inherited it, but they were born in Peru. One brother does the reception & cleans Sinks & general upkeep, ordering the groundsmen. The other brother, with his Dutch wife, who has dark hair, does the bar. So they speaks Italian & English fluently & the woman speaks Dutch & English pretty well. We seem to be the only campers who are not Dutch or Italian. There's a jar of ping pong balls in

ones reception ready for the children who playground robin on the ping pong table in the evening. The pool is fenced off but always busy.

      In the afternoon we read & clean the van. I feel back to normal now after 10 days of a bad tummy. Still need to sit down sometimes.

      When we originally got to __Camp Casio__, I said to Simon that what I really wanted to see was the jazz concert on the Saturday, but I knew that we wouldn't be here that long... But we are! And we went to the concert & it was marvellous. A very good guitar player, 2 other guitar, a, sax & a violin. They played about 6.30 - 7.45 & 8.45 - 10. BUT there was a thunderstorm

cirding the lake all thro. + it rained + rained. It hadn't rained all week + then started raining in the concert. We sat under an awning (only twice the size of our awning at home) + just stayed dry..... but then there was a mega hail storm with hails in sort of eclair shapes the size of my thumb nail. Twice this happened. We'd never seen anything like it. We chatted to nice Dutch people.

__Sunday__
We said goodbye to the campsite + set off for the Val d'Aosta. We stopped at Fénis Castle in Fénis. We also saw the local museum which exhibited crafts - woodcarvings, sayting. Fénis Castle had a St George Fresco + lovely stonework, but has been unsatisfactorily modernised in the 1930s. It rained. There was a guided tour which we couldn't understand at all: 1) rapid Italian 2) local dialect 3) talking child who shd. have been removed. But we liked the castle.
   On towards a high-up lake, Val Veny, near Mont Blanc tunnel. The Dutchman (whose photo Simon took) told us of a campsite

captions: Panorama of the Alps Mont Blanc, Giants Tooth (4014m) from [?Arete] [Hel[illegible]] (4170m)
pages.text: there. We were getting lowish on petrol (Terrific) when fortunately the road had a barrier, so we turned back + have ended

up at Camp L'Aiguille Noire (Black Peak campsite) ***. Man didn't clean back of loo properly. People nice.
__Monday__
Breakfast in the van. Not warm. As we set off on the walk to Lake Miage, Simon asked if I had my sunglasses. Half way up the hill it started to rain. It rained & it rained & it rained. We got higher & higher. We went to see where Miage glacier (near Mont Blanc) would drop chunks in a lake. The last bit was up an exposed spine of a hill. Loose stones. Thunder & lightening. Sat on rock. Drank water. Ate oatmeal Biscuits & figs. Hurried back down. Cold. Kept moving. Kind people drove us in their car, so 3 hour walk cut 15min. shorter. As we passed a chapel, the man read in Italian "Thanks be to God for Sister Water" & everyone roared with laughter.
     Back at the van my hands hardly worked. Camera broken - wet in knapsack. I had to change every garment. Only Simon's shorts wet.
     We drove throu Mont Blanc tunnel, still

captions: Le Petit Coq, Plan d'Avoz, 74430 Saint-Jean d'Aulp[s]
pages.text:
witho[ut] more petrol. We wandered round & round Morzine & I caught sight of our old ski chalet. The information said there was only a campsite at __Les Gets__. So we returned there. Still rain. __Les Frênes__ ** (Ashes).
__Tuesday__ We have [?woken] woken, in sunshine, to the most stupendous view of Mont Blanc. Sunshine on white. Nice off-road cyclists from Suffolk next door. They have opened camp swimming pool.
Just been for a glorious swim. Everything dried out & camera working again. Have even got a photo of Mont Blanc.
We drove to the baker/cake shop & walked up the drive of the house that I recognised from the Morzine ski trip (with hot tub)

At 2pm we visited Kylie Garrat & had delicious wine & rhubarb scones. She showed us photos of Chad lights that

__Le Petit Coq__, Plan d'Avoz, 74430 Saint-Jean d'Aulp[s]

she had used to decorate houses.
(One of her clients has a flat in Geneva +
a lodge at Norpine. One is Cameron Mackintosh,
who introduced her to Ridgeback dogs.)
We are camping just down the road from
her at __Baume__ municipal park (€10,
instead of the €28 we've been paying
recently.) There is a path to the river,
but no riverside path, no swimming +
the road is too dangerous to walk along.
Sunshine.
Kylee hates the cold green light of LEDs. She
likes light shining thro' porcelaine. She's used to
project management. If an arch[?itect] specifies
£170,000 she asks to see the plan. He will
only budget for, in a bedroom, a ceiling light,
2 bedside lights + 2 sockets, whereas her
spec, for rich clients, will be for a desk light +
computer, mood lights/wall lights, dimmer switches,
TV, music. So she will change the quote to
[illegible] [?million]. 275:000.
Wednesday The campsite is gloomy, in a narrow
ravine. So we moved [?out] for breakfast, shopped
at le Clerk + set off [illegible] WNW, watching Mont

Blanc as we skirted south of L. Geneva.
Across Macon. At the aire where we had
lunch, I heard a skid + crash. A car was
on its back. The passenger + a lorry
driver were behind a fence. No one
seemed badly hurt. We wove around the
lorry + car + got out just as the
emergency vehicles were arriving.
    We reach Cluny + stay at the Municipal
Campsite **** __St. Vital__ 13:[illegible] Apart from the loos
being a bit dirty, it's good + easy to get
into town + go on bike rides. After lunch we
set off to Taizé along the Voie Verte. It was
a lovely ride, mostly in the shade. At Taizé
we turned around + cycled back to
Maxilly station for water + rock cakes.
I did a bit of hand washing to get us back
to England. My shower was like warm but
Simon's was hot. There were at least 3
other English families in the campsite.
It lies between the [strikethrough: the] town, with a
view thro' the trees of the Abbey; +
the TGV, which we can just see
thro' the trees on the other side.
    We checked the internet.

Cluny Abbey    √ spire visible from campsite

[?Simbol] 9/0

__Thursday__
We moved the van to the carpark outside the campsite & walked into town to see the Abbey. Simon remembered the granary. Every thing his had to be reconstructed. We looked at 2 artificial reality screens which, as you moved

captions: Capital from the choir of Cluny 3, the beekeeper.
pages.text:
Capital from the choir of Cluny 3, the beekeeper.

[?Simbol] 9/0

them around, showed you how things had looked. The capitals & carvings are lovely. We had awful trouble in the shop. The

policy seems to be that one woman does absolutely nothing, but point people where to go. The remaining woman had a queue of at least 7 people waiting to pay for things. She was incredibly slow. Afterwards she raced out of the shop to give Simon the tiny paper receipt. It was then that Simon realised he'd left his card in the machine. [marginal annotation: Cluny is quite like Sisteron or Aldeburgh - lots of tourists, which bring life, but also a town on its own]

Lunch was delicious. I had farm chicken. Simon had pancake - Galette with goats cheese & ham. We drove NW to __Montbard__ * * ** * a municipal one with a fantastic swimming pool. Again we had problems with money. The lockers charged 20p we lost the first 20p figuring out how to use it - you stuff clothes in a barrel which revolves vertically to close. You put in the barrel no., then your 4 digit made-up no. Then put money in, then close it. When you put the nos. in at the end, it springs open. Heaven help you if you forget the nos.

The pool was amazing, indoor & out. 4 lanes for swimming in a big pool, a flume, a water shoot, battle of bubbles. Very enjoyable for everyone & free for campers. €17

For supper I did a salad - lettuce, tomato, cucumber, garlic, french dressing, cheese, pâté, lovely bread, wine, peach (v.good) apricot, plums & pastry. Simon's was meringue & butter cream, mine was a Strawberry Tart. And tea.

You can cycle from Montbard to Dijon along the canal. You can also cycle from campsite to canal, so would be a good base for cycling with a swim at the end. A bread van in the morning.

__Friday__ A long day 7-8 hours of driving, heading north. Eve drove one hour. We stopped at Le Clerke near Troyes & stocked up on wine & pâté. Sunshine. The road from Montbard → Tonnere not fast but pleasant & on to Chaouce. We reached the village next to Montreid & camped at __Le Pigeon Blanc__ * * in the village below-

places: Palais de l'Abbé Jean de Bourbon
pages.text:
Next time we cross to France we think we might camp in Kent & drive on to camp near Montreuil & wander up to the Town in the afternoon & have supper.

The loo block needs renewing. A swimming pool. The big field we camped in had one other family where the woman talked in a loud voice all the time. The woman running the site organised bingo in the evening. Her family owned it (maybe from the château, she was a law student & spent last year in Turkey with Erasmus. Why do northen French families damage campsites near Calais?

__Saturday August 11th__
We passed by St. Gabriel & Cécile & walked along a huge sandy beach - Côte d'Opale,

opat-coloured, like St. Tropez, which we passed earlier. Hideously sandy. Oh! for Suffolk shingle! Then on past Boulogne to the Eurotunnel. Our train was supposed to be 12:06. but it left at 12:13. We are wondering whether the end-of-Olympic traffic will enable us to get to the kennels before 4 pm to get Tinder. -In fact, the kennels have changed their time to 4-5, so we managed to fetd him. On the way we stopped for a cup of tea at Aston Rowant, a nature reserve for watching red kites in Oxfordshire off the M40.

A super trip.

__August 30th Friday.__     __Bourton Westwood Farm__
8.30. Simon, Eve & Tinder set off for a farm campsite **** South west of Much Wenlock. The site was set up years ago by the aunt of the present owner's [wife?]. The field was where the house [cow?] lived. They walked up the lane each day to milk her. Finally [they] dug a pit for a loo & put in a tip the other side of

captions: Wenlock Priory. Early (3 century church, Priory + Norman chapter house
pages.text:
[Photo: aerial view of ruins, captioned in rotated text along right edge: Wenlock Priory. Early (3 century church, Priory + Norman chapter house]

the field + took in campers. tt I have finished a course of Antibiotics. The doctor decided my 6 weeks of diahorea thru Italy + France was giardiasis (sp?). Horrible.
__Saturday Sept 1st__ [symbol] We went on a 4 [?mile] round trip to Much

Wenlock, Simon chose brown wool for a waistcoat + I bought bootees + a hat for Argony's new baby. Do Calcutta babies wear bootees? We had tea + homemade cake in the church. (Luch) Wenlock Priory, Cluniac, St. Milburge, is beautiful + peaceful. It was enormous. At the lavabo, 16 monks could wash at once. [strikethrough] We had lunch in the garden at the Talbot. Blackberry pudding + apple sauce. Walking home we realised we had crossed a field with a bull.
	We had a nap + then went blackberrying. But the blackberries weren't very good.
	During supper we watched the sun set on one side of the van + the moon rise on the other. Beautiful. Then we went on an amazing moonlit walk + sat on a bench + we could see the lights of Wolverhampton + fireworks.
__Sunday__
	We walked to Callaughton on foot paths + roads. Tinder ran ahead on a path

& get into a field of sheep. Ghastly. Simon just caught him before he chased a horse. Dreadful. We had lunch & left. A good camp site but poor blackberrying.

__Friday September 28th__
	Simon, Eve & Tinder left at 5 to go to __Devizes__ to spend the w/end with Charles & Jenny (arrived 2.15. Sat pm) & John (arrived 3.15. Sat.).
	We arrived about 8 & had bacon sandwiches. We're at __Foxhangar__ where in the 70's, as the canal was being repaired, a farm diversified into camp site & large marina. Lovely big open spaces. Distant sound of road. Clean loos.
__Saturday__ With Tinder on a harness we cycled up an enormous flight of locks 1796-1810. to Devizes & looked around the town. Nice, Georgian, shabby Cheltenham. We had coffee & cycled back to the van & [juked]

[Glued-in leaflet: "Explore the Magic of Our Kennet & Avon Canal" — Kennet & Avon Canal Trust, with aerial photograph of a flight of canal locks. Anniversary badge '50' visible.]

our campsite

nearly half a bucket of blackberries. After John arrived we cycled back up the locks & back again. We had booked supper at the Bear for 7.30. In the town, earlier, we had chatted to a man selling wind turbines from Iddea.
	I cycled the 11k to [?Denies] & back but Simon didn't need to cycle. He just got towed by Tinder who seems to be part huskie.
	We had a good evening meal. John had 16oz rare T bone steak. Charles had 8oz steak. Pollock, duck, sardinos
	__Sunday__
	The night wasn't as cold as the [strikethrough: per] previous one. We gave Jenny a hot water bottle. After breakfast we cycled 6½ miles towards Bradford, but there was a head wind so we didn't get there - We turned back. Tinder refused to ride in the knapsack or bike bag & we didn't think he should be running so

far, but he shows no sign of lagging behind. After elevenses at the van we all left.
	We went north west along small roads. I miss map read so we went down a very steep hill instead of [?thro] [?for] Frocester. We stopped for a bit of food in the van on the way home.

__Friday May 17th.__
	__Simon__, Eve + Tinder set off at 4.30 for Wales to see John. We are staying at __Grawen__ * * * [?symbols] *
near a road but not too noisy. Good clean loos & showers. A bike ride to beside a dam. [strikethrough: Saturday] [?We] cycled [?8]k with Tinder along reservoir. I cycled up hill & hurt my insides & they ached all day. John arrived after 11 & we went to lunch at The Station Hotel, Cefn Coed. Then we walked. We saw a water fall. Lots of up hill & down hill & I fell off at tree into a bog. Very boggy. A relief to get back to John's car. The distillery, Penderyn, was over booked. Sunshine in the evening. I've had [illegible] panadol. John's

given me nurofen, Simon has given me 2 glasses of Cremant de Bourgogne & it's wonderful to have no pain.
	Tinder is awful at pulling on the lead when we're near sheep.
	Topiaried hedges with peacocks. The land lady & husband (who died 2 years ago) have been there 50 years.
	A DoE Bronze group were there.

__Saturday~~ Sunday__
	Lovely sunny morning. We sat & chatted & read after breakfast. The DoE children formed a line & swept thro meadow looking for tith litter. After coffee we walked across the reservoir bridge. Then we had lunch & left. Tinder was behaving better by the end of the holiday.

__Saturday May 25th__ Peter's birthday.
11 Simon, Eve & Tinder left to go to Ansted so that Annette can chat with her executors, Kate (with baby Milo) & Eve. Kate, Annette & Eve meet on the Saturday. Pernille joined

us on Sunday. We discussed Annette's care while Simon went on a bike ride. After Sunday lunch we head towards Richard Holroyd in Cambridge. He was not in. So we made a cup of tea & drove home.

__Saturday June 15th__
9.40. we leave for the south of England. We visit Standen, the Beale family home, a lovely Arts & Crafts house 1880. The interior is furnished as if from 1920. Mrs. Beale & 2 of her daughters sewed magnificent wall hangings, bedspreads, cushions & chair seats. In the dining room

some of the chairs have a mythical beast in very small tapestry in the middle, sewn by the Royal College of Needlework & the Beale women sewed the surroundings. Philip Webb designed the house, Morris & Co did many of the textiles, William de Morgan ceramics. The bedroom has really lovely Gimson furniture, 4 or 5 pieces. [?Voyag-] Nice grounds. We got lost in the woods & nearly arrived late at Janny's mother's house. §Sonja lives in a big house at Capel in a huge house divided into 14 flats.
	There's a swimming pool & tennis court. lovely big airey rooms & views of woods
R & L [box]   We had a good meal & slept [?counter] in the van on the drive.
	__Sunday__ we went to Mass with Sonja St. John's Horsham. Great noisy children at the back, a nice service.
	Off to __Partridge Green__ where we are on a __Farm__ *** with easy cycling onto the Downs Link cycle path.

pages.sketch_desc: Glued-in printed map showing a terrain cross-section of the Downs Link route, with place labels from west to east: Shoreham, South Downs, Clay Vale, Greenwood Rdge, Southwater, [illegible], Cowfold, High Weald, North Downs, Guildford. A dashed line marks the cycle route. Handwritten annotation reads 'we cycled 10k & 10k back'. Printed caption reads '— Route of the Downs Link'.
pages.text:
[glued-in map: Route of the Downs Link, showing hills/terrain cross-section with places labelled: Shoreham, South Downs, Clay Vale, Greenwood Rdge, Southwater, [illegible], Cowfold, High Weald, North Downs, Guildford. Handwritten annotation on map: "we cycled 10k & 10k back" and printed caption "— Route of the Downs Link"]

It was a particularly pleasant bike ride because it didn't have demon [f]ast cyclists. They are out on the downs. There were quite a few families, an old lady & man with a dog in a push chair & even Batman & Robin. The cycle route was a railway line axed by Beeching. We could see Lancing College

[glued-in business card: The Steyning Tea Rooms / Open 7 days a week 10-6 / www.steyningtearooms.co.uk / 32 High Street / Steyning / West Sussex / 01903 810103 / Kim Cook / Proprietor / info@steyningtearooms.co.uk]

chapel in the distance. At Steyning we had a stupendous tea - rhubarb scones, rhubarb jam & then set off home, back to the van

We reached Capel about 6 & spent the evening at Sonja's flat. Sonja & Jenny gave us supper. Charles brought champagne to celebrate the end of Jenny's exams.
__Monday__
   We set off for Petworth. We shopped in the town for lunch tomorrow. (Un-pleasant deli-keeper) then looked at the house. Amazing paintings, wood carvings, kitchens. Helpful NT volunteers. Originally the house was the Duke of Northumberland's hunting lodge. When Queen Elizabeth called the Percys to live in the South, where she could keep an eye on them, they decided to develop the lodge into a home.
   We had lunch in the van & then walked in a part of the

captions: Petworth House & Park. W. Sussex. Warming side cupboard with sliding doors & hotplate on top.
pages.text:
[continuation of left page text visible in gutter — not repeated]
[Photo: kitchen interior showing copper pots on shelves and black sideboard]
Petworth House & Park. W. Sussex. Warming side cupboard with sliding doors & hotplate on top.

captions: Petworth limewood [?ext] linewood Grinling Gibbons 1690 carvings
pages.text:
Petworth
limewood
[?ext]
linewood
Grinling
Gibbons
1690
carvings

enormous Capability Brown gardens/
pa[r]k, with Timber.
	We tried to see a bit of Salmon's
Bridge Farm, the Campins home that
Simon visited when he was 10, then
continued on to Jane in Churt.
	We had supper & slept at
Longwood House
__Tuesday__
	Simon drove us to the factory,
Ash Vale & Jane & Eve caught the
train to go to the Light box
exhibition on Gertrude Jekyll 1843-1932.
We met up with Julie Aldam for 20
minutes. She pointed us on our walk
to Kate Ruby's House. Simon got there
before us. Kate was there with Dora
& Mylo who slept all the time.
	After lunch we drove Jane back
to Churt & went on to Surs [Jew]

captions: Tulips & Roses with tracings made preparatory to embroidery. Drawing by Gertrude Jekyll.
pages.text:
to admire the new flat that Charles' landlord is preparing for him. We had supper at the White Horse with Charles + Jenny + drove on home.

[photo/postcard glued in: Tulips & Roses with tracings made preparatory to embroidery. Drawing by Gertrude Jekyll.]

__Friday 28th June__  2.35.
Simon, Eve, Peter, Nana, Conrad (3½ m) + Tinder get off for Hay on Wye.  We arrive, set up the tent & walk down to the town past the 3 Tuns for a drink.  Headyswith Cider.  As we cross the bridge back to the campsite John Gaskell said "Hello Eve!"  He was out for his evening walk.  He came back to supper.  __Rodnors Farm__***** clean.

Peter & Nana & Conrad slept in the van, Simon & Eve in the tent.

__Saturday__
We walk up to the bookshops, browsed, ate Shepherd's icecreams bought food at the food festival.  At 11 we had coffee with Mrs. Gaskell & John, saw John's mental health allotment.  He gave us 2 lettuces.  We had lunch in the van & napped.  Peter dragged us off to see the map shop before it shut.  We bought maps of the areas of [?Crunachie], [?Swased] & [?Bham] canals  We had cider/beer in a lovely garden &

Kler + Nana gave us supper in the Swan Hotel. Very good. Conrad has been behaving very well.
	Sunday we packed up + left to watch at the Glad Mountain Gliding Club. We saw about 3 planes take off, [?spun] Fantastic vviews + [?ridge]. ([?we forgot [illegible]to])
	On for fish + chips in Abergavenny. On to Raglan Castle. Lovely sunshine.
	Then on to home.
__Tuesday [?7/6]th August__
	10 we left home + dropped Tinder off at kennels. The trafic + cars in the waiting area at the Channel Tunnel were lighter than usual.
__Wednesday 7th August__
	We camped next to a lake in a residential camp site, without paying at [?Humancourt] ** between [?Arras] + Cambrai.
	We walked in the woods in the evening.
	The lake was stipled with rain while we had breakfast + it hasn't stopped

raining during our trip on to St. Quentin.
	We visited the wrong big church first + then found the Basilica. Its onomous + vertical.

[Photo: labyrinth floor mosaic]

__Labyrinthe at St Quentin__

__Picardie__
__Catthedrals__

[Small map showing: Amiens, Beauvais, Noyon, Senlis, Laon, St Quentin, Soissons]

After leaving St. Quentin, Simon found a headlight was not working. We drove on to la Barbuise Campsite [illegible] (where we had stayed many years before) & had a walk over chalk fields! Near Troyes.
Thursday
  Simon can't mend the headlight because he's got a different one in the stores. In the end he discovered it was a broken fuse. We travelled south, stopping at an old church on the way which was closed. At Troyes we went to an Auchan shops, OK but a bit down market. We bought a la Reine pizza for lunch which was good, then carried on to Les Treilles Campsite [illegible] Montbard. Charles & Jenny arrived at 4 pm, so we had more tea & put up the tent. Then we went on a bike ride into Montbard. Eve bought athletes foot cream & Charles chose grey (wool) cotton for Eve to knit him a scarf. It's a good campsite - town & shops, restaurant & bike trail (Canal de Bourgogne) & free swimming.
Friday We went on a [illegible] 15 mile bike ride

La forge
de
Buffon

along the Canal & went to Buffon to see the Forge, about 1760, started by Buffon when he was 60. He spent his early life as a Naturalist, categorising birds. Then he built an amazing Forge

with beautiful architecture. Water-wheels powered 3 forges, tilt hammer, washing minerals, bellows. Part of the area is privately owned. We had bread & salami. Then we cycled to Aray+ the only food was delicious patisserie (prunes) We cycled on to Nuits sous Ravières, where we waited 30 min for a train back to Montbard. 17 miles
  Then we swam (too cold) Jenny & Eve also had to rig up the internet on their mobs (free, but we first had to download a film about Pampers.) Tricky. Simon & Eve had trouble with the lockers at the pool. Eve stood shivering because she cdn't work the codes without her glasses which were in the locker. (The following day I cdn't open the washing machine to get the

clothes out.)
  After a rest we went out to a glorious supper. Simon & Jenny had swordfish, avocado & grapefruit. We all had pork.
~~Friday~~ Saturday
  We packed up & drove to Abbey de Fontenay.

[photo: Galerie nord du cloître]
[photo: Notre-Dame de Fontenay (XIIIème siècle)]

It's one of the best places I've seen. Now lived in by the Montgolfier (Balloon descendants) family. The tilt hammer was
[photo: Vue aérienne]
invented he[re] in 1190 or 129[0]

On to Intermarché (actually we went back to ou[r

We arrived at __Ste Marie sur l'Ouche__ &
hung the washing & it dried. The weather is
now warmer & it's not threatening rain.
~~Saturday~~ Sunday
We cycled 27 miles, along the Canal de
Bourgogne away from Dijon to Eclus
Pont de Pany. We had lunch at Bryony's.
An amazing set-up next to the canal by
an English woman who spoke fluent French.
The food was just what we needed, but as
Charles said - English & not good. Simon had
ham & chips, Eve fish fingers & chips, Jenny
chicken nuggets & chips & Charles quiche &
chips - Not a tomato or green veg in sight!
The coffee was good. Jenny had forgotten her
sheepskin saddle cover & had a sore bum.
Eve had a sore bum even with the cover.
Limericks at Supper.

Monday
We set off before Jenny & Charles. They maybe
going to visit Paia. We went towards Sens
for a supermarket & the on to Provins.

We cycled past lots of Ecluses, lock-keepers houses 40[illegible]

[Photo: postcard glued in, captioned "En Bourgogne" — showing a lock-keeper's house beside a canal with mist on the water]

We reached an unusual campsite, a bit like
Center Parks, around gravel pits, Hermes Loisirs
also 'La Fontaine' [illegible] at Gouaix.
There's a bar & one of the pits is a
swimming pool. After tea we rode our
bikes around the site & had a drink at the [bar?]

It's very peaceful + green + pretty with the water. There's a remote control boat. I have shut the van doors so that Simon doesn't fall out when he falls asleep over his book.

Tuesday Simon woke me very early to roll over + look at the mist on the lake. It was very beautiful.
We drove on to Provins. It's only an hour's drive from Paris + has lots of ladies with lots of make-up. It's an old Mediaeval town, very pretty. We didn't see either of the sites (there are 4 main ones) but walked up the hill to Caesar's Tower (taken by the English in the 100 Year War who built a wall around it.).

It's octagon on a square base. We had a very good lunch – pizza + goat's

cheese + honey + crème brulee.

We continued north thru Reims to Bourget Commin. The campsite has a small indoor swimming pool + is friendly. There's a noisy family nearby with a child who doesn't stop crying wolf + screaming like a peacock. We read alot. __La Poste__

Wednesday
I got out of bed + into the pool + washed my hair. After breakfast we cycled. Simon has downloaded Russian military maps off the internet. These Russian maps have contours which are useful for planning routes that avoid steep hills. With the Russian maps there is another layer showing the Open Street Map which is a free world map generated by volunteers and this is good for footpaths and cycling routes. [?F]
We set off, shopping at Carrefour, much more cheerful than Auchan. We had lunch on the carpark. We went off on cycle ride along by fields + woods + canal, trying to stay level. There was an Italian [strikethrough: cemeti] cemetery + a British one. It was a [?sunger] ride. We got back for lunch. Then we read. We had

Supper in the restaurant of the camp-site.
There were nice people on the pitch near us — Eena & Christopher. He is organist at Freibourg. They have a smart, modern, yellow caravan.

Thursday
We set off & stopped at Carrefour, more cheapful than Auchan, lunch on the carpark. At about 2 pm we stopped at Abbeville, outside the old address of au pair Florence. Her mother invited us in. The family don't speak English. Florence now lives in Calais, but is on holiday in Corsica. Florence's mother is Genevieve. Her friend was there (a man) & grandmother & 2 nieces, Lucinda (15) & a 6 year old. Lucinda wants to visit England. Her mother died 3 years ago. The 6 year old is fun. The grandmother's first child died aged 1 in a bombardment. The second died of cancer 3 years ago. Very sad. The girls live in

Perpignan & are on holiday at their grandmother's.
Genevieve chatted to Florence on the phone, gave us a list of restaurants at Le Crotoy & booked us into her cousin's campsite at Novelles.
We reached the campsite & found a Chinese WW I Cemetry right next [to] it, so I took photos for Mark O'Neil.
__Aire Haies de Novelles__ *** very clean loos but unisex. :" Sheep & a donkey nearby but quiet campers. So odd that the Chinese Labour Corps were treated as prisoners, but have a magnificent Lutyens graveyard, [it] just like the Brits.

Friday
We went to get a map at the station, cycled to Le Crotoy. Not a very nice [bike] ride, next to a road. The tide was out all the time we were there (11.45–3) & it was interesting to watch people on the sand/mud in the bay.
We ate at Modo Modo. Very good food but
